content.full_text:
August 12, 1991


Heather C. Mc Laughlin
Assistant City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney, Room 314
City Hall
Santa Clara Avenue at Oak Street
Alameda, CA 94501

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-141

Dear Ms. Mc Laughlin:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the duties and responsibilities of City of Alameda Planning Board Members Linda Pritchard and Ralph Appezzato under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Your request does not address a specific decision before your agency but rather seeks general guidance with respect to a variety of redevelopment decisions. Accordingly, we provide you with informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2

QUESTIONS

1. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require City of Alameda Planning Board Member Linda Pritchard to disqualify herself from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of the city's proposed redevelopment plan and implementation of specific redevelopment projects?


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 2


2. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require City of Alameda Planning Board Member Ralph Appezzato to disqualify himself from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of the city's proposed redevelopment plan and implementation of specific redevelopment projects?

CONCLUSIONS

1 and 2. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require City of Alameda Planning Board Members Linda Pritchard and Ralph Appezzato to disqualify themselves from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of the city's proposed redevelopment plan and implementation of specific redevelopment projects if it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on their financial interests.

FACTS

The City of Alameda Planning Board (the "board") is composed of seven members. The city charter requires a majority vote of the seven members for the board to take action. Duties of the board include decisions regarding the city's proposed redevelopment plan for the Business and Waterfront Improvement Project. The city's general calls for the preparation of four specific plans for this project. Each specific plan involves land in the redevelopment area. The scope of each specific plan will vary according to the perceived needs of the areas involved. Generally speaking, the plans will address urban design, economic and transportation issues, and land uses.

The board is in the process of appointing members to a committee to work on specific plans for certain areas of the city. It is expected that the specific plan committee will be made up of three members of the board and three members of the city's Economic Development Commission. The specific plan committee will formulate recommendations to the board regarding the scope of work for each specific plan and scheduling priorities. After these tasks are completed, the committee will cease to exist. The specific plans will be prepared by planning consulting firms. The plans will be presented to the board, which will in turn make a recommendation to the city council concerning adoption of the plans.

The Business and Waterfront Redevelopment Area includes two major older commercial and industrial areas of the city. Overall, the proposed area comprises about ten percent of the city's total geographic area and fifty-eight percent of the city's commercial and industrial areas.

Major projects proposed for the entire redevelopment area include commercial building rehabilitation and facade improvements, transportation improvements, storm drainage projects, curb and gutter improvements, public parking

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 3

improvements, expansion of civic facilities, recreational open
space, and low and moderate income housing improvements.

Financial Interests of Planning Board Member Linda Pritchard

Ms. Pritchard is a member of the board. We understand that
her term as president expired in July of this year but she will
continue to serve as a member of the board. Ms. Pritchard is a
real estate broker by profession. She owns twenty-five percent of
the real estate company for which she works and may receive a
percentage of the sales commissions earned by other agents in the
company. The company does business in a building it owns and
which has a fair market value of between $10,000 and $100,000.
This building, located at 2515 Clement Street, is in the
redevelopment area. The land is zoned M-2, General Industrial
(Manufacturing). This zone allows most uses except residential.

Ms. Pritchard specializes in residential property sales.
Some of the sales for which she receives commissions may involve
real property situated in the redevelopment area.

Ms. Pritchard also owned residential rental property located
on 9th Street near the redevelopment area. As of October 10,
1989, and as part of a divorce settlement, she no longer owns nor
has any interest in this property.

Financial Interests of Planning Board Member Ralph Appezzato

In March, 1990, Mr. Appezzato sold his residence located on
Yorkshire Place to Anthony and Christina Poggio. Yorkshire Place
is not in or near the proposed redevelopment area. The Poggios
executed a promissory note in the amount of $153,000 to the
Appezzatos for a portion of the purchase price. The promissory
note is secured by a deed of trust on the Poggio's former
residence at 1612 Wood Street. Under the terms of the promissory
note, the Poggios have been making payments of interest to Mr.
Appezzato. The payments exceed $250 in the last twelve-month
period.

In February, 1991, Mr. Appezzato and the Poggios renegotiated
the terms of the note to extend the due date, increase the
interest rate, and provide additional security for the note. The
additional security is a deed of trust on the Yorkshire Place
residence. The amended note provides that the principal shall be
due in full upon the earlier of October 1, 1994, or the sale of
the Wood Street property. In addition, the parties agreed that in
the event of foreclosure, Mr. Appezzato would foreclose on the
Wood Street property before he foreclosed on the Yorkshire Place
property. The Poggios are currently using the Wood Street single-
family residence as rental property and receive rental income from
it.

The Wood Street residence is located in a primarily
residential area and there are no redevelopment projects planned

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 4

for this area. While located outside the proposed redevelopment
area, this single-family home is within 300 feet of a portion of
the redevelopment area which connects the major segments of the
redevelopment area.

Wood Street connects to Lincoln Avenue which is in the
redevelopment area. In this area, Lincoln Avenue is zoned R-2
with a small block of C-1. The C-1 (Neighborhood Business) zone
allows limited commercial uses which serve the nearby residential
zones. At present, this area of Lincoln Avenue is only
developed with single-family residential uses. The only
redevelopment project proposed for this area would include
replacement of minor areas of curb and gutter improvements several
blocks east along Lincoln Avenue.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest. Members of the City of Alameda's
Planning Board are public officials. (Section 82048.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a
member of the official's immediate family,³ or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

_______________
3 An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 5


     (e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

     For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

                              Section 87103.

     For purposes of the Act, an interest in real property
includes:

     ....any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest
or an option to acquire such an interest in real
property located in the jurisdiction owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public
official, or other filer, or his or her immediate
family if the fair market value of the interest is
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more....

                              Section 82033.

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision

     A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 18700(b). Additionally, a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

     (1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or

     (2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 6

     (A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of judg-
ment on the part of the official or designated
employee and the purpose of which is to influence
the decision; or

     (B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official or designated employee and the purpose
of which is to influence the decision.

                              Regulation 18700(c).

     With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

     Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
financial interests of Board Members Pritchard and Appezzato will
be materially affected by a decision, they must not only
disqualify themselves from participating in formal decisions of
the board which may affect such interests, but they must also
abstain from attempting to influence such decisions by
communicating with other members of the board or the staff
regarding the decisions.

Foreseeability

     The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198. The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

Materiality

     Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interest in a decision is
materially affected by a decision. If the official's economic
interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1
provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality.

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 7


However, when the official's financial interests will be
affected indirectly by a decision, the appropriate standards for
determining materiality are those of Regulations 18702.2 through
18702.6. When an official's interests in real property are
indirectly involved in a governmental decision, the appropriate
standard for determining materiality is that of Regulation
18702.3. These regulations are discussed below.

Effect of the Redevelopment Plan

The purpose of any redevelopment plan is to promote sound
development and redevelopment of blighted areas. The anticipated
result of redevelopment is an increase in property values and an
improved business climate within the project area, which benefits
the community as a whole. The very nature of redevelopment
projects has led the Commission to find that it is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be a financial effect on real property
values and business interests located within or near project
areas. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71; Letter No. A-81-509; Phillips Advice Letter, No. A-87-166;
Armento Advice Letter, No A-90-499. See also Downey Cares, supra,
at 983.) Thus, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of
redevelopment decisions on the public officials' interests will be
material, disqualification is required.

Analysis of the Financial Interests of the Public Officials

Board Member Pritchard

Board Member Pritchard has a number of potentially
disqualifying financial interests located within the redevelopment
project area. She owns 25 percent of a real estate company. The
company does business in a building it owns which is located in
the redevelopment project area. This real property is valued at
between $10,000 and $100,000. Because her interest in the
business exceeds 10 percent, sources of income to the business are
deemed sources of income to Ms. Pritchard. (Shaw Advice Letter,
No. A-90-119.)

We now proceed to analyze each financial interest
individually to determine whether Board Member Pritchard must
disqualify herself from participating in decisions regarding the
proposed redevelopment projects.

1. Real Property Interests

You have stated that Ms. Pritchard no longer holds any
interest in residential rental property located in the
redevelopment project area which she owned prior to October 10,
1989. For purposes of our analysis, we assume that she in fact
has no interest in this property. We caution, however, that if
Ms. Pritchard has received payments in excess of $250 in the
preceding twelve months deriving from the sale of this real
property, or if she holds a deed of trust or any other ownership

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 8

interest in this real property, both the real property and the
source of payments, if any, should be analyzed for purposes of
compliance with the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.

We turn now to the building owned by the real estate business
in which Ms. Pritchard has a 25 percent interest. This building
is located within the redevelopment project area. As
discussed above, redevelopment decisions tend to improve property values in
the area. Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable that
decisions regarding the redevelopment area will have an effect on
the value of this building. We must now determine whether the
effect of such decisions will be material.

The effect of a decision on an official's interest in real
property is material if:

     (D) The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or any of
the above decisions; and real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

     (E) For purposes of this subdivision, the
terms "zoning" and "rezoning" shall refer to the
act of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the subject property, but shall
not refer to an amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses permitted, or the
standards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to all other
properties designated in that category.

Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).

Pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18702.1, the effect
of a decision on real property in which a public official has an
ownership interest of $1,000 or more is material when the decision
has a direct effect on the real property. Redevelopment decisions
are deemed to have a direct effect on real property situated
within the redevelopment area. Accordingly, redevelopment
decisions are presumed to have a material financial effect on
property situated within the redevelopment area.

2. Business Interests

Ms. Pritchard owns a twenty-five percent interest in the real
estate company where she is employed. This business entity will

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 9

be affected indirectly by redevelopment decisions. When an
official has an economic interest in a business entity indirectly
involved in the decision, the appropriate standard for determining
materiality is that of Regulation 18702.2. For all but the
largest business entities, the applicable standards are those of
Subdivision (g) of Regulation 18702.2 which states that the effect
of a decision upon a business entity indirectly involved in a
decision is material if:

(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Regulation 18702.2(g).

If a decision will affect a business entity in which an
official has an ownership interest in the amounts set forth above,
the effect of the decision is material.⁴ We have insufficient
information to make this determination. Ms. Pritchard must assess
the potential effect of redevelopment decisions upon this business
interest. Among the factors to be considered in making this
evaluation is whether the redevelopment decisions will result in
higher commissions for this business as the value of real property
in the redevelopment area increases. If she determines that the
effect of such decisions will be material, she must disqualify
herself from participating in such decisions.

In the Oglesby Opinion, supra, the Commission examined the
redevelopment issue in terms of two types of economic interests
which might disqualify the councilmember in question from
participating in a decision to adopt the proposed redevelopment
plan. These economic interests included the councilmember's
ownership interest in three lots located within the redevelopment
area and his business as a real estate broker active in the
redevelopment area.

__________
⁴ For purposes of our analysis we assume that this business
entity is not publicly traded and does not meet any of the other
standards set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)-(f). If our
assumption is incorrect, please apply the appropriate subdivision
in Regulation 18702.2 to determine whether the effect of the
decision is material.

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 10

The Commission concluded that the proposed redevelopment
would have a material financial effect on the councilmember's real
estate business because such a business earns its income from
commissions based on a percentage of the value of the property.
When property values increase, the amount of the commission income
increases.

The Commission stated that:

     The eventual sale or lease transactions
involving any properties within or outside the area
will result in increased commissions, proportional
to the increased values, to Mr. Whatley. There is
also the likelihood that the increased attractions
added to the downtown area and the upgrading of
other neighborhoods by new access and street
improvements will result in greater numbers of
property transactions. Both these possibilities
constitute a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Councilman Whatley's real
estate business, since both the size and the number
of commissions generated by real estate sales in
the area are likely to increase. In addition, if
the redevelopment plan is adopted, it is possible
that Mr. Whatley's official position on the
Redevelopment Agency would make available to him
inside information which could result in a
financial benefit to his real estate business.
Even if he did not, as we presume he would not, use
confidential information gained from his official
activities for private business purposes, potential
customers might believe he would be privy to such
information and thus more effective as a real
estate agent. (Emphasis added.)

     Oglesby, supra, pp. 10-11.

     Board Member Pritchard's situation is somewhat analogous to
the situation discussed in Oglesby. Ms. Pritchard owns twenty-
five percent of a real estate company which does business in the
redevelopment area. We have previously advised that real estate
businesses located within or near redevelopment areas are in a
unique position to benefit from redevelopment decisions. These
businesses, and the professionals affiliated with them, earn their
income from commissions based on a percentage of the value of the
property sold. It is precisely this connection between
redevelopment decisions and likelihood of increased income to
real estate businesses located in the area which provides the
requisite foreseeability that Board Member Pritchard will benefit
in a manner distinguishable from the public generally. (See
Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm. and In re
Oglesby, supra.)

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 11

Accordingly, absent facts to indicate that the effect of
redevelopment decisions on Board Member Pritchard's business will
not be material pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18702.2,
it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the adoption
of the city's proposed redevelopment plan and implementation of
specific redevelopment projects will have a material financial
effect on the real estate business in which Board Member Pritchard
holds a twenty-five percent interest.

3.  Sources of Commission Income

Ms. Pritchard has received commission income from her own
real estate sales and also from sales completed by agents working
for the real estate brokerage firm of which she holds a 25 percent
interest. These commissions may also create disqualifying
financial interests. Regulation 18704.3 provides that the
following are deemed to be sources of income to a public official
within the meaning of subdivision (c) of Section 87103:

     (A) The person the broker represents in the
transaction;

     (B) If the broker receives a commission from
a transaction conducted by an agent working under
the broker's auspices, the person represented by
the agent;

     (C) Any brokerage business entity through
which the broker conducts business; and

     (D) Any person who receives a finder's or
other referral fee for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.

Regulation 18704.3(c)(2).

In addition, if Ms. Pritchard is acting as a broker for the
sale, purchase, or lease of real property, she may, in certain
circumstances, be the recipient of "promised" income within the
meaning of Subdivision (c) of Section 87103. Commission income is
deemed "promised" income to a real estate broker when the sale is
pending (i.e. the sale is in escrow). (Larsen Advice Letter, No.
A-82-192; Armento Advice Letter, No. I-90-032.)

If any of the sources of income mentioned above will be
materially affected by redevelopment decisions, Ms. Pritchard must
disqualify herself from participating in such decisions. The
standard for determining materiality with regard to an individual
who is a source of income of $250 or more to a public official in
the twelve months preceding a decision and who is indirectly
involved in a governmental decision is that of Regulation 18702.6
which states that:

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 12


The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official if any of the following applies:

(a) The decision will affect the individual's
income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or

(b) The decision will affect the individual's
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under Section 18702.3 or
Section 18702.4.

Regulation 18702.6.

Please note, however, that if the source of income is a
business entity indirectly involved in the decision, then the
applicable standards for determining materiality are those of
Regulation 18702.2 discussed above. This would be the case if a
business entity had been a source of commission income to Board
Member Pritchard directly or to the real estate business in which
she holds an ownership interest.

Board Member Appezzato

Board Member Appezzato holds a promissory note in the amount
of $153,000 on a personal residence located in the City of Alameda
but outside the redevelopment project area. This financial
interest is not disqualifying for purposes of the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act.⁵ However, this promissory note is
secured by a deed of trust on a single-family residence located
within 300 feet of a portion of the redevelopment project area.
The purchaser of this home is making payments in excess of $250 in
a twelve month period to Mr. Appezzato. Accordingly, Mr.
Appezzato has two financial interests which may be affected by
redevelopment decisions: (1) a source of income within
jurisdiction (the Poggios) and (2) an interest in real property
located within 300 feet of the redevelopment project area.

1. Source of Income

Under the terms of the promissory note on the Yorkshire Place
residence, the Poggios have been making payments of interest to
Mr. Appezzato. These payments exceed $250 in the preceding twelve


_____________________

5 For purposes of our analysis we assume that this residence is
not located within 2,500 feet of the redevelopment project and
will not be affected materially by redevelopment decisions.

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 13

months. Thus, the Poggios are potentially disqualifying sources
of income. As discussed above, in order to determine whether this
source of income is disqualifying, you must first determine
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial interests
of either of the Poggios will be affected by redevelopment
decisions. For example, if Mr. Poggio conducts business within
the redevelopment area, it is foreseeable that redevelopment
decisions may have a financial effect upon his business.
Secondly, you must determine whether the effect will be material
both on Mr. and Mrs. Poggio as individuals and on their business
interests they may have within the jurisdiction. The appropriate
standards for determining materiality are those of Regulations
18702.6 and 18702.2, as discussed above. Finally, you must
determine whether the Poggios' real property interests in the
vicinity of the redevelopment area will be affected materially as
discussed below.

     2.  Real Property Interest

     The Poggios are the owners of a single-family residence
located within 300 feet of the redevelopment project area on which
Mr. Appezzato holds a deed of trust. For purposes of our analysis
we assume that the Poggios' interest in this property exceeds
$1,000. Mr. Appezzato's deed of trust on this residence also
constitutes an interest in real property. (Section 82031,
Phillips Advice Letter, No. I-90-340.) As discussed above,
redevelopment decisions tend to improve the value of real
property. Accordingly, it is foreseeable that the value of this
residence will be affected by redevelopment decisions. If the
effect of redevelopment decisions on the value of this single-
family residence will be material, Mr. Appezzato must disqualify
himself from participating in such redevelopment decisions because
such decisions will have a material financial effect on the real
property interests of the Poggios who are sources of income to Mr.
Appezzato and on Mr. Appezzato's interest in the property.

     You have indicated that this single-family residence is
located within 300 feet of a secluded portion of the proposed
redevelopment area where little redevelopment activity will take
place. However, public improvements to street rights-of-way are
contemplated as projects in this area. Because this real property
will not be affected directly by redevelopment decisions, we
determine materiality by applying the standards set forth in
Regulation 18702.3 which states in pertinent part that the effect
of a decision is material if:

     (1) The real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official's real property interest.

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 14


     (2) The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.

          Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) and (2).

     Pursuant to Regulation 18702.3, the effect of decisions on
real property situated within 300 feet of property which is the
subject of the decision is presumed material. Therefore, Mr.
Appezzato is required to make a reasonable good faith effort to
determine the financial effects on the single-family residence on
which he holds a deed of trust. Factors which must be considered
in determining whether a decision will have a material financial
effect on the real property include, but are not limited to:

          (1) The proximity of the property which is
     the subject of the decision and the magnitude of
     the proposed project or change in use in
     relationship to the property in which the official
     has an interest;

          (2) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
     the decision will affect the development potential
     or income producing potential of the property;

          (3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
     of residential property, whether it is reasonably
     foreseeable that the decision will result in a
     change to the character of the neighborhood
     including, but not limited to, effects on traffic,
     view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
     emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.

          Regulation 18702.3(d).

     If it is determined that redevelopment decisions relating to
areas situated within 300 feet of the single-family residence on
which Mr. Appezzato holds a deed of trust will have any financial
effect on the value of this single-family residence, 
disqualification is required unless the "public generally"
exception applies, as discussed below.

Public generally

     Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in the same manner, disqualification may be required

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 15

unless the effect of the decision on the public official's
financial interests is the same as the effect on a significant
segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.) For purposes of our
discussion, the public is the entire population of the city. (In
re Legan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77;
Joergensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017.)

Board Member Pritchard

We conclude that the "public generally" exception is
inapplicable to Ms. Pritchard. This is so because her ownership
interests in a building and in a business entity within the
redevelopment area will be affected in a manner which is not
similar to the effect of the decisions on the general public. We
do not have any facts that would indicate that either a large
percentage of the population of the City of Alameda owns
commercial real estate within the redevelopment area or that a
substantial segment of the public will benefit materially by
redevelopment decisions.

Board Member Appezzato

With regard to Mr. Appezzato's interests, we also conclude
that the "public generally" exception does not apply. This is so
because it does not appear from your facts that a significant
number of single-family residences are located within 300 feet of
the proposed redevelopment area and will be affected in a manner
which is substantially similar to the effect on the residence in
which Mr. Appezzato holds a financial interest.

Bifurcation

Under certain circumstances, large and complex decisions may
be divided into separate decisions when an official has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decisions which is
not interdependent upon other components. However, under some
circumstances, a series of decisions may be too interrelated to be
considered separately. (Miller Advice Letter, No. I-82-119.)
This would be the case if a decision on one aspect of a
redevelopment plan will substantially affect a decision on another
aspect of the plan. Under such circumstances, if an official must
disqualify himself or herself from participating in one of the
decisions, he or she is also disqualified from participating in
the other decision.

For example, in a decision to select one of two autopark
sites, a decision to select one of the sites essentially a
decision against the other autopark site. (Boogaard Advice
Letter, No. I-90-347.) Similarly, decisions regarding the street
improvement projects located within 300 feet of the residence in
which Mr. Appezzato has a financial interest may be so
interrelated to other decisions that they may not be bifurcated
because one decision will have an effect on other related
decisions.

Our File No. I-91-141
Page 16

However, if it is possible to consider one particular aspect
of the redevelopment plan independently of the others, and if the
items are not interrelated, they may be bifurcated. If certain
items of the redevelopment project may be bifurcated, then Board
Members Pritchard and Appezzato would be required to abstain from
participating in discussions of disqualifying aspects of the
redevelopment plan while participating freely in other decisions
regarding the plan.

If you determine that certain decisions regarding the
redevelopment plan are not so interrelated that decisions
regarding one component will affect other decisions, you may
bifurcate such decisions and consider them separately from other
elements of the proposed redevelopment plan. In order to
bifurcate certain issues, the board must adhere to the following
procedure:

(1) The decisions in which a board member has a
disqualifying financial interest must be segregated from other
decisions.

(2) The decisions from which a board member is disqualified
must be considered first, and a final decision must be reached by
the board without the participation of the disqualified board
member.

(3) Once a final decision has been reached on a particular
item, a disqualified board member may participate in the
deliberations and vote regarding other areas of the redevelopment
plan so long as those deliberations and vote do not affect the
previous decisions from which he or she was disqualified.
(Mengel ff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542; McNatt Advice Letter, No.
I-90-714.)

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Bianca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.first_500_words: August 12, 1991 Heather C. Mc Laughlin Assistant City Attorney Office of the City Attorney, Room 314 City Hall Santa Clara Avenue at Oak Street Alameda, CA 94501 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-91-141 Dear Ms. Mc Laughlin: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the duties and responsibilities of City of Alameda Planning Board Members Linda Pritchard and Ralph Appezzato under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Your request does not address a specific decision before your agency but rather seeks general guidance with respect to a variety of redevelopment decisions. Accordingly, we provide you with informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2 QUESTIONS 1. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require City of Alameda Planning Board Member Linda Pritchard to disqualify herself from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of the city's proposed redevelopment plan and implementation of specific redevelopment projects? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File No. I-91-141 Page 2 2. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require City of Alameda Planning Board Member Ralph Appezzato to disqualify himself from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of the city's proposed redevelopment plan and implementation of specific redevelopment projects? CONCLUSIONS 1 and 2. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require City of Alameda Planning Board Members Linda Pritchard and Ralph Appezzato to disqualify themselves from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of the city's proposed redevelopment plan and implementation of specific redevelopment projects if it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on their financial interests. FACTS The City of Alameda Planning Board (the "board") is composed of seven members. The city charter requires a majority vote of the seven members for the board to take action. Duties of the board include decisions regarding the city's proposed redevelopment plan for the Business and Waterfront Improvement Project. The city's general calls for the preparation of four specific plans for this project. Each specific plan involves land in the redevelopment area. The scope of each specific plan will vary according to the perceived needs of the areas involved. Generally speaking, the plans will address urban design, economic and transportation issues, and land uses. The board is in the process of appointing members to a committee to work on specific plans for certain areas of the city. It is expected that the specific plan committee will be made up of three members of
